advaita.rendering: For the protection (paritrāṇa) of those established in the right path (sanmārga-stha) and the destruction of the sinful (pāpakārin), Īśvara — who in reality undergoes no true birth — appears age after age through māyā. The third purpose subsumes both: by removing adharma and reinstating Vedic dharma, the apparent Lord restores the very conditions under which jñāna can arise in qualified seekers. The avatāra is thus not a cosmic rescue but an epistemological clearing — the real work remains the seeker's own discrimination.
advaita.key_divergence: Śaṅkara subordinates the protective-and-destructive acts to dharma-saṃsthāpana as the epistemic purpose; the avatāra clears the path to jñāna, it does not itself liberate.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: parirakṣaṇāya sanmārgasthānām ... dharmasya samyak sthāpanaṃ tadartham

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: The sādhus here are specifically the Vaiṣṇava devotees (mat-samāśrayana-pravṛtta) who, unable to bear even a moment's separation from Bhagavān's form and deeds (nāma-karma-svarūpa), become undone in body and limb — for their paritrāṇa (rescue from that unbearable longing), Bhagavān descends with his form, speech, and glance as nourishment. The duṣkṛtas are not merely sinners but those opposed to this devotional dharma; their destruction too serves the bhaktas' welfare. Descent is an act of love in every age, unrestricted to any particular yuga.
viśiṣṭādvaita.key_divergence: Rāmānuja defines sādhus as advanced bhaktas in acute devotional longing, not merely virtuous persons; paritrāṇa is the relief of devotional suffering, not merely protection from harm.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: aṇumātrakālam api kalpasahasraṃ manvānāḥ praśithilasarvagātrā ... mat-svarūpa-ceṣṭitāvalokana-alāpādi-dānena teṣāṃ paritrāṇāya

dvaita.rendering: There is no rule that birth alone accomplishes protection and destruction; Hari acts by his own svabhāva (intrinsic nature) and by līlā (spontaneous play), entirely free from compulsion. Like a child at sport — yet the child is Anantavīrya, of boundless power — he who is pūrṇa (utterly full) and has nothing to gain still undertakes all action; the seers who know the highest and the lowest (parāvara-jña) declare him to act out of apparent contradiction, which is itself the proof of his absolute independence (svātantrya). Paritrāṇa and vināśa are the sport of the supremely full One.
dvaita.key_divergence: Madhva foregrounds Hari's absolute svātantrya and pūrṇatva — descent is not need-driven but pure līlā; quotes Brahma-Sūtra 2.1.33 and Ṛgveda-khila to establish play as the sole motive.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: na janmanaiva paritrāṇādi kāryam iti niyamaḥ ... līlayā svabhāvena ca yatheiṣṭacārī ... pūrṇo'yam asya na kiñcid āpyam tathāpi sarvāḥ kurute pravṛttīḥ

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Verse 4.7 named the when; 4.8 names the purpose — but in Puṣṭi-mārga the tripartite purpose is entirely Kṛṣṇa's own prasāda-impulse: he protects the dharma-vat (those who possess dharma), destroys the duṣkṛt (those whose karma is corrupt), and does so yuge yuge without calendrical constraint. Even the apparent severity of destruction carries no pitilessness (nairghṛṇya): as a mother who both caresses and strikes her infant acts without cruelty, so Maheśa — the supreme controller of virtue and fault — acts solely from niyantṛ-love. The descent is both when-he-wills and how-he-wills.
śuddhādvaita.key_divergence: Vallabha explicitly deflects any imputation of harshness in vināśa with the mother-infant analogy shared with Śrīdhara; the timing ('yuge yuge') refutes any fixed-yuga rule, centering Kṛṣṇa's sovereign will.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: nāvatārakālaniyama ity etadarthaṃ yuge yuge uktam ... lālane tāḍane mātur nākāruṇyaṃ yathārbhake

bhakti.rendering: The question 'for what purpose?' (kim-artham) is answered in three clean steps: protection of the sādhus (those who walk svadharma), destruction of the duṣkṛtas (evil-doers), and through both acts together the firm establishment of dharma (dharmaṃ sthīrīkartum). Śrīdhara, like Vallabha, adds the mother-analogy to remove any charge of cruelty against the destroyer-function. The descent is occasioned not by yuga-sequence but by the arising of the need (tat-tad-avasare) — an on-demand, responsive avatāra theology.
bhakti.key_divergence: Śrīdhara reads paritrāṇa and vināśa as jointly instrumental to dharma-saṃsthāpana — neither is the terminal purpose; descent is responsive and occasion-triggered, not calendrically determined.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: sādhurakṣaṇena duṣṭavadhena ca dharmaṃ sthīrīkartuṃ yuge yuge tattad-avasare sambhavāmīty arthaḥ

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana opens with a dialectical challenge: would not the very occasion of dharma's decline and adharma's rise — the pretext for avatāra — make the descent itself a source of harm? He refutes this by unpacking the three purposes as a single coherent act: paritrāṇa means protecting from every side (paritaḥ sarvato rakṣaṇam) the sādhus on the Vedic path now imperiled; vināśa removes those actively opposing that path; and dharma-saṃsthāpana is precisely the preservation of veda-mārga by the removal of adharma. The avatāra is therapeutically complete — diagnosis, excision, and restoration in one descent.
advaita-bhakti.key_divergence: Madhusūdana's gloss on 'paritrāṇa' as paritaḥ sarvato rakṣaṇam (protection from every direction) is unique; his dialectical framing answers the objection that avatāra in a fallen age compounds the fall rather than cures it.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: paritrāṇāya paritaḥ sarvato rakṣaṇāya ... dharmasya samyag adharma-nivāraṇena sthāpanaṃ veda-mārga-parirakṣaṇaṃ dharmasaṃsthāpanam

so_what_questions: If avatāra is māyā-mediated (Advaita) rather than a real birth, what moral weight does its protective-destructive function carry — can an unreal agent truly protect or destroy?
Rāmānuja defines the sādhus as devotees in acute separation-longing: does this restrict the promise of paritrāṇa to advanced bhaktas, or does it simply reveal the deepest form of the general promise?
Madhva insists descent is pure līlā with nothing to gain — yet the verse lists three purposes. How does Dvaita reconcile purposive language with the doctrine of Hari's absolute pūrṇatva?

All six schools agree 'yuge yuge' means no fixed-yuga rule. What does an on-demand, occasion-responsive avatāra theology imply for communities living through apparent periods of adharma without a visible descent?

The dharma-saṃsthāpana purpose is shared across schools yet filled with different content: Vedic path (Śaṅkara, Madhusūdana), Vaiṣṇava bhakti-dharma (Rāmānuja), svadharma-observance (Śrīdhara). Whose dharma is being established?

Vallabha and Śrīdhara both invoke the mother-analogy to neutralize the nairghṛṇya (cruelty) charge against vināśa. Does this analogy dissolve the moral problem or merely relocate it into a relationship of asymmetric power?

If protection and destruction are two faces of a single dharma-restoring act, is there a reading of this verse in which the duṣkṛta is also, in some sense, being served — freed from the karma that was destroying them?

advaita.application: When you take on a protective or corrective role — parenting, teaching, managing — act cleanly from dharma, not from emotional investment in outcomes. Śaṅkara's avatāra clears conditions for jñāna; your interventions should clear conditions for others' clarity, then step back. The moment you believe you are the rescuer, you have mistaken the instrument for the source.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: When someone you love is in acute longing or suffering — the Rāmānuja sādhu model — your 'descent' is to show up with presence, story, and voice (svarūpa-ceṣṭita-āvalokanā), not with solutions. The care that relieves devotional-style longing is relational and sensory, not merely transactional. Schedule the visit, make the call; paritrāṇa here is showing your face.

dvaita.application: Act from your deepest svabhāva (innate nature) rather than from need for recognition or fear of failure — Madhva's Hari acts because it is his nature, not because he lacks anything. Identify the work that flows from your nature effortlessly (as the child plays), and do it fully. The fact that you have nothing to gain is the condition, not the obstacle.

śuddhādvaita.application: In a community or family role, trust that corrective action — even firm, uncomfortable correction — is not a betrayal of love if it flows from the niyantṛ (governing) function. The mother who disciplines is not cruel. Vallabha's frame: the sign of authentic care is that both the lālana (nurturing) and the tāḍana (correction) come from the same undivided intention.

bhakti.application: Recognize that your interventions — in an organization, a neighborhood, a family system — become dharma-saṃsthāpana only when protection of the good and removal of the harmful are coordinated, not pursued separately. Śrīdhara's reading: neither sādhu-rakṣaṇa alone nor duṣṭa-vadha alone establishes dharma — both must be present, occasion-responsive, and proportionate.

advaita-bhakti.application: When entering a situation of visible decay — an institution losing its dharma, a community fraying — adopt Madhusūdana's therapeutic completeness: diagnose what is threatened (sādhus), identify what is actively corrosive (duṣkṛtas), and focus your energy on restoring the underlying structure (veda-mārga, the generative principle). Partial interventions that protect without removing the corrosive element, or remove without restoring the structure, are incomplete descents.

self_assessment: Six distinct doctrinal renderings anchored in supplied bhāṣya prose with real divergence across all schools; Śrīdhara rendering uses Sanskrit content only, avoiding any HTML artifacts from payload; seven so-what questions probe cross-school tensions; everyday applications are functionally distinct; all bhāṣyas were present and used directly.
